title: Semester Course Curriculum

questions.question: What are the primary areas of focus in HIST 3050 that students will study?
What is the primary teaching method used in HIST 3050?
choices: B. Interactive group projects and case studies
C. Fieldwork and observations of historical sites
D. Simulation exercises based on real-world scenarios

questions.question: What is the purpose of HIST 3050 in the course curriculum?
choices: A. To introduce upper division students to critical thinking skills
B. To provide a comprehensive overview of political systems and governance
C. To delve into historical events with an interdisciplinary approach
D. To foster analytical thinking through case studies

title: Instructor Information

questions.question: What is the name of the course instructor?

title: Learning Objectives

questions.question: 1. Define and critically analyze competing definitions of "terrorism" and "political violence"
2. Trace the historical evolution of terrorist tactics, ideologies, and organizations from antiquity to the 21st century
3. Evaluate the philosophical and ethical debates surrounding the use of political violence

title: Grading Breakdown

questions.question: What is the weighted percentage of Attendance & Participation in the course?
How many components are included in this section's assignment breakdown? (Answer '1', '2', or '3')
What is the weighted percentage of Primary Source Analysis Paper?

title: Grading Scale

questions.question: What is a common letter grade between 83 and 86?
Which of the following letter grades is closest to an F (below 60)?

title: Sensitive Content Warning

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a graphic historical event described in this course?
The study of mass casualty events helps students understand what?
What method is used to engage analytically with difficult material in this course?

title: Academic Integrity

questions.question: What constitutes plagiarism according to the course guidelines?
choices: A. Failure to cite sources correctly
B. Accidental copying of someone else's work
C. Submitting a previously submitted assignment without proper citation
D. Failure to accurately paraphrase content

title: Respectful Dialogue

questions.question: Which method of argumentation is most effective in fostering respectful dialogue?
choices: D. Focused analysis of the topic

questions.question: To engage with a politically charged issue effectively, it is best to:
choices: A. Avoid discussing sensitive topics entirely
B. Seek common ground and compromise at all costs
C. Prioritize one's own interests over others' rights

questions.question: Students should approach ideologies they find repugnant with:
choices: A. A critical eye, but no empathy for those who hold them
B. Sympathetic understanding and willingness to listen
C. A dismissive attitude and refusal to engage
D. Analytical rigor while maintaining personal opinions

title: Wednesday — The Problem of Definition

questions.question: What term is used to describe deliberate targeting of civilians by non-state actors for political ends?
Which definition describes the use of terror as a means of achieving a state's political objectives against its own population?
Who is labeled as 'terrorists' when their actions are defined under this category, regardless of the context or motivation behind them?
choices: Those they claim to represent or target for political reasons
Governments and international organizations with a vested interest in labeling others as such

title: Monday — Who Becomes a Terrorist? Psychological Profiles

questions.question: Which of the following terms is associated with Moghaddam's Staircase Model?
In Moghaddam's model, which psychological state leads to the willingness to use violence and terrorism?
choices: A: Moral engagement with terrorist group

questions.question: Which of the following is a significant aspect of the Root Causes Debate in understanding terrorism?
choices: B: Humiliation as a key factor
C: Identity crisis and cultural belongingness

title: Wednesday — Group Dynamics and Radicalization

questions.question: In which location did Mohamed Atta and the Hamburg Cell undergo their radicalization, excluding any obvious pre-existing signs of violent intent?
Which author's work on terrorism networks would you require to understand the dynamics at play in groups like the Hamburg Cell?
What are some of the psychological factors that contribute to radicalization, as described by Sageman's research on terrorist networks?
choices: Pre-existing social networks and personal relationships, group cohesion, charismatic leadership.
Destitution, poverty, and material needs, lack of belonging, and disillusionment with societal norms.
Traditional religious values and cultural heritage, community involvement and social support.
Personal ambition and desire for status or power, exposure to radical ideologies in the media.

title: Monday — State Terrorism Through History

questions.question: What was the original meaning of the word "terrorism" and who coined it?
choices: A. It originally referred to violence by non-state actors.
B. It was first used by the Committee of Public Safety during the French Reign of Terror.
C. It was a term used by Napoleon Bonaparte.
D. It originated in ancient Greece

questions.question: How many people were executed under Robespierre's Committee of Public Safety (1793–94)?
What is the distinction made by some historians between 'state terrorism' and 'non-state terrorism', as per Ariel Merari's reading?
choices: A. State terrorism involves government actions that result in fewer deaths.
B. Non-state terrorism refers to violence carried out by non-governmental groups.
C. State terrorism is characterized by a focus on state actors, while non-state terrorism focuses on international organizations.
D. State terrorism is defined as the use of violence against one's own people and non-state terrorism involves violence directed at foreign targets

title: Wednesday — Counterterrorism and Civil Liberties

questions.question: Which British law created internment camps in Northern Ireland during the late 1960s?
choices: A. The Prevention of Terrorism Act, 1974
B. The Prevention of Terrorism Act, 1971
D. The Prevention of Violence Act

questions.question: What U.S. Supreme Court case established the right to detain people without due process?
Which modern case involves a person detained under the PATRIOT Act, and what issue has been argued over their treatment?
choices: C. Rasul v. George W. Bush

title: Monday — Terror in the Ancient World

questions.question: What is the name of the Greek term for a dagger or short sword, often used by the Sicarii to commit their assassinations?
Who is credited with being one of the first recorded terrorists, using mass atrocities as military strategy?
In what year did the Sicarii begin their activities according to Josephus?

title: Wednesday — The Assassins: Medieval Islamic Terror Network

questions.question: Which Muslim scholar wrote about the origins and ideology of the Nizari Ismailis, describing them as a splinter group from the Fatimid dynasty (909–1171 CE)?
According to Lewis, what was Hassan-i Sabbah known for and where did he establish his stronghold?
choices: A. Strategically located near an important trade route
B. Highly skilled in the art of assassination
C. A charismatic leader with a vision for a new Islamic caliphate
D. A master builder with expertise in architecture

questions.question: What was the nature of the Nizari Ismailis' use of targeted assassination, and what was its impact on politics during their time?

title: Friday — The Spanish Inquisition and Religious State Terror

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a key feature of the Inquisition as an institution of social control through fear?
choices: A. Torture, confession, and public spectacle (auto-da-fé)
C. Persecution of witches and Jews

questions.question: The intent to commit religious terrorism is a distinguishing characteristic between prosecution and which other activity?
choices: A. Terrorism as an end in itself

questions.question: In what way was the role of intent crucial to distinguishing prosecution from terrorism during the Inquisition?
choices: A. Intent to harm individuals without provocation.
B. Intent to protect one's beliefs from perceived threats.
C. Intent to destroy property as a means of intimidation.
D. Intent to spread fear and disorder among the population

title: Monday — Anarchist Ideology and the "Propaganda of the Deed"

questions.question: According to Bakunin, what is the relationship between ideas and deeds?
choices: Ideas result from acts, not the latter from the former.
Deeds result from actions, not the latter from the former.
Both are equally important, as they lead to change.

questions.question: What was Pisacane's reasoning behind his concept of 'propaganda of the deed'?
choices: Destruction is often used in warfare, and it can be argued that war itself is a form of propaganda.
It does not lead to change because ideas result from acts, not the latter from the former.
It results from a combination of both; destruction can communicate more than a thousand pamphlets.

questions.question: How did Kropotkin describe the philosophy of destructive violence?
choices: Destruction is often used in warfare, and it can be argued that war itself is a form of propaganda.
It does not lead to change because ideas result from acts, not the latter from the former.
It results from a combination of both; destruction can communicate more than a thousand pamphlets.

title: Friday — The Assassination That Changed the World: Sarajevo, 1914

questions.question: Which terrorist group did Gavrilo Princip associate with and what were their goals?
What was the name of the group that assassinated Archduke Franz Ferdinand on June 28, 1914 and what were their tactics?
What is the term for the combination of circumstances that escalated the assassination into a war, including political interests, nationalistic tensions, and military unpreparedness?

title: Monday — Irish Republicanism and the IRA (1916–1940s)

questions.question: What British intelligence group did Collins infiltrate and destroy, leading to a temporary blind in British operations in Ireland?
choices: D. The Bureau of Military Justice

questions.question: What methodical approach did Collins employ to dismantle British intelligence networks, leading to the eventual blindness in British operations?
choices: A. Limited himself to assassinations of key targets only
B. Used advanced technology for surveillance and infiltration
C. Employed guerrilla warfare tactics on a large scale
D. Focused solely on urban assassination campaigns

questions.question: What was Collins' approach to targeting British agents, which helped establish the modern concept of urban guerrilla warfare?
choices: A. Selective killing of civilian populations
B. Targeting government officials and military personnel only
C. Indiscriminate terror tactics against civilians
D. Focusing solely on targeted assassinations of key targets

title: Wednesday — Zionism, Palestinian Nationalism, and the British Mandate

questions.question: The Irgun and Lehi (Stern Gang) were Jewish terrorist groups; what was their primary target?
What happened during the Deir Yassin massacre?
choices: It resulted in a British military response and an end to the violence.
The Irgun and Lehi were disbanded after this event.
A ceasefire agreement was signed between Israel and Palestine nationalists.
The massacre triggered widespread Palestinian support for Palestinian nationalism.

questions.question: Who is responsible for comparing Palestinian fedayeen tactics with the birth of Israel in order to justify displacement?

title: Friday — Mao, Marighella, and the Theory of Revolutionary Terror

questions.question: What is the primary argument in Frantz Fanon's *The Wretched of the Earth* that justifies violence against the colonizer as a means of restoring the colonized person's humanity?
choices: A: Colonization leads to economic exploitation
B: The need for decolonization is urgent
C: Violence can be used to liberate oneself from colonial rule
D: The psychological wound suffered by colonized peoples requires violent liberation

questions.question: What does Sartre's preface in *The Wretched of the Earth* endorse regarding revolutionary violence?
choices: A: It is necessary for achieving justice and equality
B: It promotes the dehumanization of oppressors
C: It leads to social and economic inequality
D: It justifies violent conflict between rival ideologies

title: Monday — The New Left and the 1960s–70s Terror Surge

questions.question: What was the term for student radicalization in the global 1968 moment?
Which group claimed responsibility for hijacking Lufthansa Flight 181 and demanded the release of imprisoned RAF leaders?
choices: B. Baader-Meinhof Group / Red Army Faction

questions.question: What was the turning point in West German counterterrorism when GSG 9 commandos stormed a hijacked plane?
choices: A. The capture of the hijacker
B. The release of the hostages
C. The death of the hijacker
D. The capture of the RAF leader

questions.question: Which country did not experience a significant left-wing terrorist operation in this section, and what was it?

title: Wednesday — Right-Wing Terrorism: Neo-Nazism, Militias, and Christian Identity

questions.question:
According to historian Kathleen Belew's book, who was Robert Mathews leading in the early 1980s?
A. A group of young men from a troubled community seeking revenge for their family's murders.
B. A militia group preparing for an armed uprising against government control.
C. The Knights Templar, resurrected as an underground neo-Nazi network.
D. A cult leader promoting the teachings of Louis Beam.

title: Friday — State-Sponsored Terrorism During the Cold War

questions.question: Which organization received U.S. support for anti-communist insurgencies in Central America during the 1980s?
choices: The South African National Liberation Army

questions.question: Which Libyan regime was accused of supporting terrorism, and for what reason?
choices: Gaddafi's pan-Arab nationalism led to increased state accountability after the Lockerbie bombing.
Gaddafi's support for anti-communist insurgencies in Central America made him a target of U.S. policy change.
Gaddafi's regime was known for supporting Palestinian resistance, but this had no impact on U.S. actions during the Cold War.
Gaddafi's regime was accused of sponsoring terrorism without justification or provocation from Washington.

questions.question: Which Pan-Arab nationalist movement led by a former PLO leader was supported by the CIA, and for what reason?
choices: The Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
The Palestine Liberation Organization as an independent entity

title: Wednesday — Hijacking and the Internationalization of Terror

questions.question: The first wave of aircraft hijackings (1968–1972) involved which group that was known for targeting military planes?
What four simultaneous hijackings of aircraft took place in Dawson's Field in Jordan before international cameras?
choices: A. Four US Air Force planes.

questions.question: What direct consequence of the international response to Dawson's Field came about during 'Black September'?
choices: A. Jordan's economy was heavily impacted by international sanctions.
B. The US government responded with increased military aid to Israel.
C. Jordan and Israel signed a peace treaty, leading to relative stability in the region.
D. Israel launched a devastating campaign against Palestinian resistance.

title: Friday — **Primary Source Analysis Paper** Due

questions.question: In analyzing the primary source selected by a student, what is considered essential when examining historical context?
choices: A. The author's personal opinion or bias
B. The location where it was written and published
C. The intended audience of the text
D. The date the document was created

questions.question: What is one way to assess the ideological framework present in a primary source, according to the Course Reader?
choices: A. By evaluating its tone and language usage
B. By comparing it to other sources on similar topics
C. By analyzing its influence on historical events
D. By looking at how it relates to broader social issues

questions.question: What should a student consider when determining the intended audience of their primary source analysis?
choices: A. The author's age and occupation
B. The publication date, format, and language used
C. The historical time period in which it was written
D. The social class or economic status of its creator

title: Monday — Historical Roots of the Conflict (1169–1969)

questions.question: In what year did the English begin colonizing Ireland, and under what circumstances?
Which event in 1921 partitioned Ireland into two new countries, leading to the formation of Northern Ireland?
What significant movement in the 1960s highlighted ongoing discrimination and inequalities faced by Catholics, leading to violent protests?

title: Friday — The Peace Process and Lessons of Negotiation

questions.question:
During the 1981 hunger strikes, what was the primary goal of Sinn Féin leaders?
A. To increase their negotiating power
B. To gain political recognition from British government
C. To force IRA to abandon violence
D. To attract new recruits for armed struggle

Which party transitioned from being a paramilitary group to participating in elections and forming the Northern Ireland Assembly?
A. Continuity IRA
B. Ulster Defence Association
C. Sinn Féin
D. Irish Republican Army
What is one key lesson the Good Friday Agreement model has for conflict resolution across the globe?

title: Monday — Midterm Review Session

questions.question: What is a theoretical framework in the context of social and political thought?
choices: A. A set of rules for logical reasoning.
B. A philosophical stance on human nature.
C. A method for analyzing complex systems.
D. A scientific theory explaining natural phenomena.

questions.question: Which wave refers to the anti-colonial and Cold War movements in the Caribbean and Latin America?
Which technological advancements were pivotal during the Troubles, such as sniper rifles and explosives?

title: Wednesday — IN-CLASS MIDTERM EXAM

questions.question: 1. Compare the philosophical justifications for political violence offered by Fanon and Walzer.
2. Trace the evolution of terrorism from the Anarchist Wave to the Cold War.
3. Using the case study of Northern Ireland, analyze what conditions allow a terrorist movement to transition to peaceful politics.

title: Monday — Soviet-Afghan War and the Mujahideen (1979–1989)

questions.question: What was the primary ideological justification for the mujahedeen's resistance against the Soviet Union?
Who were the 'Afghan Arabs' and what role did they play in shaping the jihadist movement?
choices: B. Saudi Arabian sheikhs and students
C. Uzbek warlords and regional commanders

questions.question: Who was Osama bin Laden's mentor in the jihadist movement, and what did he believe formed the basis of the mujahedeen's cause?

title: Friday — 9/11: Planning, Execution, and Immediate Aftermath

questions.question: Who was the operational planner of 9/11?
In what country were the leaders of the Hamburg Cell based?
What was the timeline of events leading up to and including the day itself on September 11, 2001?
How did Al-Qaeda's strategic goals change in response to the attacks by American forces and coalition partners? *(Course Reader)*
choices: Maintain focus on attacking Western targets

title: Monday — Afghanistan, Iraq, and the Limits of Military Counterterrorism

questions.question: What was the primary goal of Operation Enduring Freedom when it began in 2001?
choices: D. Decentralization after the fall of Saddam Hussein

questions.question: Which organization, founded by Osama bin Laden, continued to conduct attacks after the invasion of Afghanistan in 2001?
What does the acronym 'Iraq War decision' refer to, and what was its alleged justification for invading Iraq?
choices: C. Ousting the Taliban in Afghanistan
D. Al-Qaeda attacks on US soil

title: Wednesday — Drone Warfare, Targeted Killing, and the Ethics of Remote War

questions.question: What was the first non-reconnaissance platform to be armed with Hellfire missiles?
Who is credited with raising unprecedented legal and ethical questions about sovereignty, due process, and proportionality in the context of drone warfare?
What percentage of strikes were conducted by CIA's drone program outside military chain of command, leading to unprecedented questions about sovereignty, due process, and proportionality?

title: Friday — ISIS: The Caliphate as Terrorist State

questions.question: What is the name of the AQI leader who founded ISIS, and what innovation allowed for the creation of a terrorist state within three years?
Who declared the Caliphate, leading to its proclamation by al-Baghdadi and a subsequent declaration of war against the Islamic State?
What are two aspects of ISIS's dual nature as terrorist organization and functioning proto-state?
choices: A. Terrorism and governance, media and propaganda
B. Governance and terrorism, recruitment and extremism
C. Media control and ideological indoctrination
D. Extremist ideology and territorial conquest

title: Wednesday — Aum Shinrikyo and Apocalyptic/Doomsday Terrorism

questions.question: What is the name of the cult behind the 1995 Tokyo subway attack?
How many people were killed in the 1995 Tokyo subway attack?
Which topic focuses on cult psychology and organizational dynamics within apocalyptic groups?

title: Monday — The Evolution of Terrorist Technology: A Historical Overview

questions.question: Which 19th-century technology enabled widespread democratization?
What did Alfred Nobel's legacy as a philanthropist include, in addition to his work on explosives?
In which region did the first car bombs emerge, and what was their purpose?

title: Wednesday — Cyberterrorism and Information Warfare

questions.question: Which country likely deployed the first major state-level cyber assault in 2007?
The term 'cyberterrorism' was first coined to describe what type of attack?
Which group is often referred to as the 'Cyber Caliphate' due to their attacks on Western targets and dissemination of Islamic teachings online?

title: Friday — Bioterrorism: History, Threat Assessment, and Prevention

questions.question:
Which of the following is NOT a historical example of biological warfare in East Asia?
A) The Mongol Empire's use of plague-ridden catapulted corpses to spread disease during the 13th century
B) Unit 731's experiments on humans at the behest of Imperial Japan
C) A 1948 attack by North Korea on South Korea with anthrax spores
D) The 2005 outbreak of avian flu in China

What is the connection between the Rajneeshee salmonella attack and Unit 731?
A) Both were state-sponsored biological warfare programs
B) Both used similar pathogens for bioterrorism
C) Both targeted a U.S.-backed government
D) Both had links to the U.S. military

In what year was the 2001 anthrax letters (Amerithrax) attack, and who claimed responsibility?
A) 2001: David Leigh and Jim Acosta
B) 2007: Army lab scientist Bruce Ivins
C) 2005: Former Soviet scientist Ken Alibek
D) 1984: Rajneeshees attacking Rajneesh temple

Which of the following is NOT a consequence of research advancing dual-use biology?
A) Development of vaccines to prevent biological warfare
B) Increased risk of unintended release of pathogens in biolabs
C) Wider availability of pathogen strains for agricultural use
D) Greater awareness about the ethics and limitations of biotechnology

title: Wednesday — Counterterrorism Strategy: What Works?

questions.question: Which method of counterterrorism was used most in the RAND study (Jones & Libicki, 2008)?
What percentage of terrorist groups ended through military means as per the RAND study?
Which method was used by only 7% of terrorist groups according to the RAND study?

title: Friday — The Future of Terrorism: AI, Drones, and Unknown Threats

questions.question: What topic discusses the use of AI to develop synthetic pathogens for bioterrorism?
What scenario involves a lone actor using AI-designed synthetic pathogen to release it in a subway system?
choices: A. Scenario A: Lone actor with AI-designated synthetic pathogen releases it in a subway system
B. Scenario B: Nation-state uses proxies and deepfakes to trigger ethnic violence in a rival country without attribution
C. Scenario C: Decentralized far-right accelerationist network coordinates simultaneous attacks across 12 cities using encrypted apps and 3D-printed weapons
D. Scenario D: Autonomous weapon systems designed for swarm warfare

questions.question: What topic discusses the use of consumer drones in terrorist activities such as attacks by ISIS or the Houthis?
choices: A. AI-enabled terrorism: deepfakes, autonomous weapons, persuasion AI
B. Synthetic biology and the democratization of WMD production
C. Quantum computing and the end of encrypted communications
D. Space, infrastructure, and emerging domains of attack

title: Appendix B: Academic Resources

questions.question: Which of these research institutions is part of a national consortium and also offers training for individuals interested in terrorism studies?
choices: START Center (National Consortium for the Study of Terrorism)
Combating Terrorism Center at West Point (CTC Sentinel)
International Centre for the Study of Radicalisation (ICSR)

questions.question: Which database provides access to over 85,000 terrorist attacks from 1970 onwards, allowing researchers to analyze trends and patterns in terrorism?
choices: Profiles of Individual Radicalization in the United States (PIRUS)
Combating Terrorism Center at West Point (CTC Sentinel)

title: Appendix C: Course Calendar Summary

questions.question: What topic is covered in week 4?
When is the Midterm Exam scheduled?

title: Appendix D: A Note on Objectivity and Analytical Distance

questions.question: Which of the following describes why understanding terrorism is essential for dismantling it?
choices: A. Ignoring it will prevent future attacks.
C. Understanding its logic makes it understandable, not excusable.
D. Comprehending its reasoning does not excuse violent actions.
